draft.introduction: Luke’s Sermon on the Plain (6:20–49) presents Jesus’ ethical teaching in a more direct, socially radical form than Matthew’s Sermon on the Mount. The blessings address the literally poor, not the “poor in spirit.”

translation.text: And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said: ‘Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are you who are hungry now, for you shall be satisfied.’
translation.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς ἐπάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ εἰς τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ ἔλεγεν · μακάριοι οἱ πτωχοί ὅτι ὑμετέρα ἐστὶν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ

translator_notes.note: Luke’s “Blessed are you who are poor” (μακάριοι οἱ πτωχοί) lacks Matthew’s qualifier “in spirit.” This has led to centuries of debate over whether Jesus’ teaching addresses material poverty or spiritual disposition.
